clusters.title: Teen shot by police after allegedly pulling out knife during welfare check
clusters.neutral_headline: Teen Shot By Police During Welfare Check

items.title: Teen shot by police after allegedly pulling out knife during welfare check
items.standfirst: Officers were called to a home on Akebia Street in Arana Hills earlier today to conduct a welfare check.
items.content: A teenager is undergoing emergency surgery after he was shot twice by police following an altercation in Brisbane's north-west.The 19-year-old man's mum called Triple Zero about 11am (AEST) to ask for mental health support for her son at their home on Akebia Street in Arana Hills, police said.Three police officers, as well as paramedics arrived at the home about 90 minutes later.NATIONAL: What to expect from prime minister's rare address to the nationInside the home, two of the officers allegedly tried to encourage the man to accept mental health support before he produced a large kitchen knife.One of the male officers, a first-year constable, fired two shots, which struck the 19-year-old in the abdomen, Detective Superintendent Andrew Massingham told media this afternoon.His mother witnessed the shooting."Mum is distressed, she is traumatised," Massingham said."The situation here is one that we encounter where we attempt to get help for somebody and unfortunately, that help doesn't always go as planned and situations like this arise where unfortunately this sort of action needs to be taken ."It's a lethal use of force option that is our last option."Officers and paramedics immediately provided first aid at the scene.The man was rushed to Royal Brisbane and Women's Hospital in a critical condition to undergo emergency surgery. CRIME: Man pleads guilty to teacher's brutal murder after remains found in freezerMassingham said a Taser was also discharged at some point, but it is unclear whether that occurred before the gun was fired."The timing of that in terms of when the shots were fired will be subject to the ongoing investigation," Massingham said.The police officer who fired the shots was "distraught", Massingham said. "We don't come to work everyday expecting this and when the reality hits that something like this has occurred, it is tramuatic for all involved and that young man is feeling that right way.Body-worn cameras were operating at the time and will be reviewed. The officers involved are yet to interviewed."We are currently in the process of doorknocking, obtaining witness statements of people that may have tragically heard or witnessed the subsequent events after this man was shot," Massingham said.The matter will be investigated by the Ethical Standards Command with oversight by the Crime and Corruption Commission, as is standard practice.Lifeline 13 11 14If you have information for police, contact Policelink by providing information using the online suspicious activity form 24hrs per day at www.police.qld.gov.au/reporting.Report crime information anonymously via Crime Stoppers. Call 1800 333 000 or report online at www.crimestoppersqld.com.au.

items.standfirst: A young man is undergoing emergency surgery after he was shot by police inside a home in Brisbane's north.
items.content: A young man is undergoing emergency surgery after he was shot by police inside a home in Brisbane's north.

Incident occurred in Arana Hills

clusters.title: Man pleads guilty to former teacher’s ‘chest freezer’ murder
clusters.neutral_headline: Man Pleads Guilty To Teacher Murder

items.title: Man pleads guilty to former teacher’s ‘chest freezer’ murder
items.standfirst: The killer was 24 at the time of the 2019 murder of a 58-year-old retired teacher, whose body was found buried in a chest freezer.
items.content: The killer was 24 at the time of the 2019 murder of a 58-year-old retired teacher, whose body was found buried in a chest freezer.

items.title: Man pleads guilty to teacher's murder after remains found in buried freezer
items.standfirst: Bobby Andrew Weaver pleaded guilty to murder seven years after body parts were found in a chest freezer buried in the victim's backyard.
items.content: A man has pleaded guilty to murder exactly seven years after body parts were found in a chest freezer buried in the victim's backyard.Bobby Andrew Weaver faced Brisbane Supreme Court today for what was meant to be a pre-trial hearing.The crown prosecutor instead asked Justice Rebecca Wilson to arraign Weaver on one count of murder.READ MORE: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese to make rare national address tonightWeaver said "guilty" when asked how he would plead to the charge of murdering David Charles Thornton some time between January 24 and March 10, 2019 at the Ipswich suburb of Goodna, west of Brisbane.Weaver declined to say anything further to Wilson.Thornton, a retired maths and science teacher, was last seen near his Goodna home on February 22 and was reported missing by his family on March 12, 2019.Police began digging in his yard over a weekend in late March.READ MORE: Fugitive Dezi Freeman believed to have been shot up to 20 times during tense stand-off with policeOn April 1, 2019 they excavated the freezer that contained human remains belonging to Thornton.Weaver was extradited to Queensland from NSW on April 3, 2019 following his arrest on the previous day at a Pacific Highway rest stop at Tyagarah, just north of Byron Bay.He was due to face the start of a Supreme Court trial on July 27 this year.Wilson today ordered Weaver continue to be remanded in custody and scheduled a review on April 8 to confirm a date for sentencing.Weaver's barrister said she would seek a subpoena for her client's medical records to assist with sentencing.

ai_summary: Man pleaded guilty to murder of a teacher
Victim's body was found in a buried freezer
Killer was 24 years old at the time of murder
Body parts were found in a chest freezer
Guilty plea came seven years after the crime

items.title: Share markets energised as they price in end to Iran war, but there's a catch
items.standfirst: If you had woken up today and heard someone say Wall Street's tech sector rose more than 3 per cent overnight, you would expect it to be promptly followed by "April Fools' Day".
items.content: If you had woken up today and heard someone say Wall Street's tech sector rose more than 3 per cent overnight, you would expect it to be promptly followed by "April Fools' Day".

clusters.title: Bluey's lead composer on helping kids fall in love with music
clusters.neutral_headline: Bluey Composer Inspires Kids

items.title: Bluey's lead composer on helping kids fall in love with music
items.standfirst: A new orchestral Bluey album is out. Lead composer Joff Bush talks about classical music, storytelling and inspiring kids to love music.
items.content: A new orchestral Bluey album is out. Lead composer Joff Bush talks about classical music, storytelling and inspiring kids to love music.

ai_summary: Joff Bush composes music for Bluey
New orchestral Bluey album is released
Bush aims to inspire kids to love music
Classical music is used in storytelling
Music education is important for kids

clusters.title: New mine owner claims he's faced threats over removing bikie shareholders
clusters.neutral_headline: New Mine Owner Faces Threats

items.title: New mine owner claims he's faced threats over removing bikie shareholders
items.standfirst: The Tasmanian government has taken steps to revoke the operating licence of one of the state's biggest mining operations, after being warned it was allegedly used as a "vehicle for money laundering" and former owners were associated with underworld figures.
items.content: The Tasmanian government has taken steps to revoke the operating licence of one of the state's biggest mining operations, after being warned it was allegedly used as a "vehicle for money laundering" and former owners were associated with underworld figures.

ai_summary: New mine owner claims to face threats
Bikie shareholders are involved
Tasmanian government revokes licence
Mine operation is allegedly used for laundering
Underworld figures are associated with owners
